sections.heading: (3) Commission Recommendation 2003/361/EC of 6 May 2003 concerning the definition of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises
sections.title: European data spaces for cross-border data sharing and re-use
sections.summary: This section establishes a framework for harmonized data exchanges across borders by creating common European data spaces in sectors like health, finance, and mobility. It mandates data governance principles ensuring data findability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability (FAIR principles) while maintaining high cybersecurity standards. The framework aims to facilitate cross-border data sharing without compromising fundamental rights or public security.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates cross-border data transfer mechanisms through sector-specific data spaces that require FAIR data principles and cybersecurity safeguards, directly impacting DTIA requirements for international data transfers.

requirements.text: Establish domain-specific common European data spaces for data sharing and pooling
Ensure data is findable, accessible, interoperable and reusable (FAIR principles)
Maintain high level of cybersecurity
Create harmonized framework for data exchanges and basic data governance requirements
Facilitate cooperation between Member States for data sharing

sections.source_quote: The Commission proposed establishing domain-specific common European data spaces for data sharing and data pooling. As proposed in the European strategy for data, such common European data spaces could cover areas such as health, mobility, manufacturing, financial services, energy or agriculture, or a combination of such areas, for example energy and climate, as well as thematic areas such as the European Green Deal or European data spaces for public administration or skills.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data sharing framework with security mandates

sections.title: Public sector data re-use: exclusions and conditions
sections.summary: This section specifies that the re-use regime applies to public sector data involved in public tasks, excluding public undertakings, cultural establishments, educational institutions, and research organizations. It mandates transparent public task definitions, requires data to be held in accordance with public law, and excludes data from organizations that are not public sector bodies. The re-use of data for non-commercial scientific research purposes among researchers is exempt from the regulation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines exclusions for public sector data re-use, clarifying when data transfers across borders are exempt from the re-use regime. It specifies conditions for public task data that may affect cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Public sector bodies should define public tasks transparently and subject to review
Public tasks should be defined generally or on a case-by-case basis for individual public sector bodies
Public undertakings data should not be covered by this regulation
Cultural establishments data should not be covered by this regulation
Educational establishments data should not be covered by this regulation
Research-performing organizations data should not be covered by this regulation
Data held by public undertakings in specific public-private associations should not be covered by this regulation
Public sector bodies should not re-use data for non-commercial scientific research purposes

sections.source_quote: The re-use regime provided for in this Regulation should apply to data the supply of which forms part of the public tasks of the public sector bodies concerned under law or other binding rules in the Member States.
sections.relevance_reason: Exclusions for public sector data re-use

sections.title: Public sector data re-use: competition and security safeguards
sections.summary: This section outlines competition safeguards for public sector data re-use, prohibiting exclusive agreements that restrict data re-use unless justified by public interest. It mandates transparency, regular market reviews, and publication of exclusive agreements. It also specifies that non-personal data transfers to third countries require equivalent safeguards, including legal remedies and enforceable rights, to protect trade secrets and intellectual property.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details specific safeguards for cross-border transfers of non-personal data, including requirements for equivalent protection in third countries and legal remedies to ensure data security.

requirements.text: Public sector bodies should avoid exclusive agreements that create competitive barriers
Exclusive agreements must be justified and necessary for public interest services
Exclusive agreements should be limited to 12 months and subject to market review
Exclusive agreements must be published online in a form compliant with public procurement law
Non-personal data transfers to third countries require equivalent safeguards
Third countries must have equivalent legal protections for non-personal data
Re-users must accept jurisdiction of the public sector body's Member State for disputes
Public sector bodies must ensure non-personal data is protected from unlawful access

sections.source_quote: Public sector bodies should comply with competition law when establishing the principles for re-use of data they hold, avoiding the conclusion of agreements which might have as their objective or effect the creation of exclusive rights for the re-use of certain data.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer safeguards

sections.heading: (3) Commission Recommendation 2003/361/EC of 6 May 2003 concerning the definition of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises (cont.)
sections.title: Data intermediation services: definition and scope
sections.summary: This section defines data intermediation services as platforms enabling data sharing between data holders, data subjects and data users without establishing commercial relationships. Member States must establish a single information point for reusing public sector data, which should be cross-sectoral and use automated means where possible. Competent bodies must support public sector bodies in data re-use, including technical assistance for data storage and interoperability while maintaining data privacy. These entities should not have supervisory functions but must comply with data protection regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Data intermediation services require specific safeguards for cross-border data sharing, including neutrality, interoperability, and no commercial relationships. They must not process personal data for commercial purposes without explicit consent from data subjects.

requirements.text: Member States should establish a single information point for reusing public sector data with cross-sector remit
The single information point should use automated means for enquiries or requests for data re-use
Competent bodies must support public sector bodies in data re-use, including technical assistance for data storage and interoperability
Competent bodies should not have supervisory functions reserved for data protection authorities
Data intermediation services must not establish commercial relationships between data holders and users
Data intermediation services must not process personal data for commercial purposes without explicit consent

sections.source_quote: Member States should establish a single information point to act as an interface for re-users that seek to re-use that data. It should have a cross-sector remit, and should complement, if necessary, arrangements at the sectoral level.

sections.title: Data intermediation services: operational requirements
sections.summary: This section outlines operational requirements for data intermediation services, including neutrality, interoperability, and compliance with competition law. Providers must ensure no conflicts of interest, maintain data privacy, and implement procedures for fraudulent practices. They must also comply with national laws of their main establishment location and follow sector-specific standards where applicable.
sections.dtia_summary: Data intermediation services must operate neutrally, ensure interoperability, and avoid conflicts of interest to facilitate secure cross-border data sharing without commercial relationships.

Data intermediation services must send a notification to the competent authority from the Member State where main establishment is located

sections.source_quote: Data intermediation services providers should act only as intermediaries in the transactions, and do not use the data exchanged for any other purpose.

sections.title: Data intermediation services provider registration and oversight
sections.summary: Data intermediation services providers must have their main establishment in the Union. Those not established in the Union offering services within the Union must designate a legal representative who acts on their behalf for compliance purposes. The legal representative must be designated via written mandate and competent authorities may address them directly for enforcement actions. The designation is necessary due to handling of personal and commercially confidential data.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data intermediation services providers to establish a legal representative within the Union for oversight, creating a cross-border compliance mechanism for data sharing activities.

sections.title: Data altruism organizations and consent frameworks
sections.summary: Recognised data altruism organizations must have a Union establishment or legal representative, be non-profit, and follow transparency requirements. They must collect data directly or process data collected by others, with consent mechanisms compliant with GDPR. Data altruism organizations should be able to process data for purposes established at consent time or allow third-party processing for those purposes.

sections.title: Penalty criteria for data intermediation services

These criteria include the nature and severity of the infringement, mitigation actions taken, previous infringements, financial impacts, and other aggravating or mitigating factors. requirements.text: The nature, gravity, scale and duration of the infringement
Any action taken by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation to mitigate or remedy the damage

The financial benefits gained or losses avoided by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation due to the infringement
Any other aggravating or mitigating factors applicable to the circumstances of the case

sections.title: Penalty criteria for data intermediation services
sections.summary: Member States must consider specific criteria when imposing penalties on data intermediation services providers and recognised data altruism organisations. These criteria include the nature and severity of the infringement, mitigation actions taken, previous infringements, financial impacts, and other aggravating or mitigating factors. This ensures penalties are tailored to the specific circumstances of each case.

requirements.text: The nature, gravity, scale and duration of the infringement
Any action taken by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation to mitigate or remedy the damage
Any previous infringements by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation
The financial benefits gained or losses avoided by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation due to the infringement
Any other aggravating or mitigating factors applicable to the circumstances of the case

sections.source_quote: Member States shall take into account the following non-exhaustive and indicative criteria for the imposition of penalties on data intermediation services providers and recognised data altruism organisations for infringements of this Regulation, where appropriate: (a) the nature, gravity, scale and duration of the infringement; (b) any action taken by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation to mitigate or remedy the damage caused by the infringement; (c) any previous infringements by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation; (d) the financial benefits gained or losses avoided by the data intermediation services provider or recognised data altruism organisation due to the infringement, insofar as such benefits or losses can be reliably established; (e) any other aggravating or mitigating factors applicable to the circumstances of the case.
